problem_statement: The document addresses whether CoT-enhanced LLM vulnerability detectors and code-auditing agents used for automated code review can reliably oversee AI-generated/upstream code, showing their reasoning chains expose exploitable weaknesses that let obfuscated malicious code bypass review.
magnitude_evidence: Malicious code 'is frequently integrated into production systems without rigorous human review or security vetting'; CoTDeceptor 'bypasses 14 out of 15 vulnerability categories, compared to only 2 bypassed by prior methods'
insufficiency_evidence: 'Manual auditing is costly, expert-dependent, and fundamentally unable to keep pace with the growing volume of code'; static analysis is 'easily evaded by minor obfuscations'; and CoT detectors show 'even the strongest LLM detectors are susceptible to reflective, multi-stage adversarial obfuscation' (e.g., Codex failed to flag the obfuscated CSRF vulnerability)
solution_mechanisms: adversarial data generation for fine-tuning detectors
notes: Paper is primarily an offensive/attack framework; its oversight relevance is documenting failure of automated LLM code-review oversight and calling for robust/reasoning-aware auditing pipelines. Some OCR garbling in tables and code listings.
